column2: Back
Each token's query is compared (dot product) to every key to get softmax weights, then takes that weighted sum of values — a content-based mixture of all positions.

column1: Why divide attention scores by sqrt(d_k)?
column2: q.k of d_k unit-variance terms has variance d_k (std sqrt(d_k)); dividing keeps logits O(1) so softmax isn't saturated and gradients don't vanish.
Compute O(T^2 * d); memory O(T^2) for the score matrix — quadratic in T.
~12 d^2: attention 4 d^2 (Q,K,V,O) + MLP 8 d^2 (up d x 4d, down 4d x d).
~2N for an N-parameter model (1 multiply + 1 add per param); backward ~2x, so training ~6N.
Modern LLMs use pre-norm: x + Sublayer(LN(x)) keeps a clean identity residual so gradients reach depth; post-norm needs careful warmup.
Encodes relative position directly in the QK dot product, adds no parameters, and supports context extension (interpolation/YaRN).

column1: Where do most transformer params live?
column2: The MLP (8 d^2) vs attention (4 d^2) — about two-thirds of block params and most stored knowledge.
Stores past keys/values so each decode step computes K/V for only the new token — O(T) per step instead of recomputing O(T^2).
Subtract max(x) before exp: result is shift-invariant, prevents fp16 overflow, and makes the largest exponent equal 1.

column1: Gradient of softmax cross-entropy wrt logits
column2: softmax(z) - onehot(y) — simply predicted minus target.

column1: One design choice behind many dumb failures
column2: Tokenization: the token (not the character) is the atom — hence poor spelling, digit arithmetic, and whitespace/glitch tokens.
About 20 tokens per parameter; scale N and D together. GPT-3 was under-trained at ~1.7 tokens/param.
~6 FLOPs per parameter per token (2 forward + 4 backward) ties compute, parameters, and tokens.
weights 2 + grads 2 + optimizer 12 (fp32 master 4 + m 4 + v 4) = 16 bytes/param.
params 14 + grads 14 + optimizer 84 = ~112 GB before activations.

column1: Why bf16 over fp16 for training
column2: bf16 has fp32's 8-bit exponent — huge range, rarely overflows, no loss scaling; fp16 (5 exponent bits) overflows easily.
Stage 1 shards optimizer state; stage 2 also gradients; stage 3 (≈FSDP) also parameters, gathered on demand.
Parameters, gradients, optimizer state, and activations.

column1: Comms pattern: TP vs PP vs DP
column2: TP: all-reduce activations every layer (intra-node/NVLink); PP: point-to-point between stages plus a bubble; DP: all-reduce gradients once per step.
Discard activations in the forward pass and recompute in backward: ~33% more compute for large activation-memory savings.

column1: Why train past the Chinchilla optimum
column2: Inference economics — a smaller, over-trained model is cheaper to serve at scale (the Llama philosophy).
e^(cross-entropy); the effective branching factor. PPL 20 = as uncertain as choosing among 20 tokens.
Freeze W0 and learn a low-rank update dW = B A added to it, training under 1% of parameters.

column1: LoRA trainable params for a d x d matrix at rank r
column2: 2 d r vs d^2; ratio 2r/d (e.g. d=4096, r=8 gives ~0.4%).
NF4 4-bit quantization, double quantization, and paged optimizers.
r(x,y) = beta * log(pi_theta / pi_ref); substituting into Bradley-Terry gives a loss on chosen vs rejected logprobs.

column1: Why mask prompt tokens in SFT loss
column2: So the model learns to generate the response, not predict the user's prompt — prevents parroting.

column1: Role of the KL penalty in RLHF
column2: Anchors the policy to the reference (SFT) model; without it the policy reward-hacks the imperfect reward model.
Fine-tuning has low intrinsic dimension — the weight update dW is approximately low-rank.
4-bit base ~3.5 GB + small adapters ~ a few GB, versus ~112 GB for full fine-tuning.
Policy exploits RM flaws for high score without quality; guard with KL penalty, early stopping, and a stronger/ensembled RM.
DPO needs paired preferences; KTO needs only binary good/bad labels; ORPO merges SFT + preference in one stage with no reference model.
Use PEFT/LoRA, lower LR, fewer epochs, and replay general data.

column1: Limits of perplexity as a metric
column2: Only measures language-model fit, is not comparable across tokenizers, and says nothing about helpfulness, correctness, or safety.
pass@k = 1 - C(n-c, k) / C(n, k), from n>=k samples with c correct.

column1: Why pairwise comparison beats absolute scoring
column2: Humans and judges are noisy on absolute scales but consistent on relative judgments — higher agreement, and it aggregates via Elo.
Position (swap order and average), length/verbosity (length-control), self-preference (use a different or ensembled judge).

column1: RAG: the two things to evaluate separately
column2: Retrieval (recall@k, MRR, nDCG) and generation (faithfulness + answer relevance).
When a measure becomes a target it stops being a good measure — optimizing a benchmark games it.

column1: How to compute a bootstrap CI
column2: Resample per-item scores with replacement n times, recompute the metric, repeat ~10k times, take the 2.5 and 97.5 percentiles.
N-gram/MinHash overlap of eval items against training data; also compare original vs freshly written equivalents.
The model's stated confidence matches its empirical accuracy; measured with ECE / reliability diagrams.
MRR = 1/rank of the first relevant result (one correct answer); nDCG handles graded relevance where order matters.

column1: The two phases of autoregressive generation
column2: Prefill (parallel prompt pass, compute-bound) and decode (one token at a time, memory-bandwidth-bound).
Each token is a tiny GEMV that must still stream all model weights from HBM — very low arithmetic intensity.
2 * L * n_kv * d_head * T * B * bytes_per_elem; linear in context length and batch.
MQA: one shared KV head (max savings, quality drop); GQA: grouped KV heads (most savings, near-MHA quality) — the modern default.
Tiles Q/K/V in SRAM with an online softmax and never materializes the T x T matrix — same math, far less HBM IO.

column1: Why speculative decoding preserves the distribution
column2: Accept a draft token with prob min(1, p_target/p_draft), else resample from the residual — provably matches the target distribution.
~4x less weight data moved per token, so ~4x faster in the bandwidth-bound limit.
Stores the KV cache in fixed-size blocks (like OS pages) to eliminate fragmentation and pack many more concurrent requests.
Single-token intensity ~2/b; batching B requests gives ~2B/b for the same weight read, moving toward compute-bound.
Time-to-first-token (prefill latency), time-per-output-token (decode speed), and total tokens/sec across requests.
Prompt to steer; RAG for changing/proprietary knowledge with citations; fine-tune for behavior/format/style.
Dense embeddings capture semantics; sparse/BM25 captures exact keywords — fuse (e.g. RRF) to cover both failure modes.
A cross-encoder re-scores top-N candidates by jointly reading query+doc — precision at the top, at the cost of latency.
Was the gold chunk retrieved? If yes it's a generation/grounding issue; if no it's retrieval (chunking, embeddings, or query).
Large chunks keep context but add distractors; small chunks are precise but split answers; overlap mitigates at a storage cost.
Interleave Reason (thought), Act (tool call), and Observation (result) in a loop until the model can answer.
Step/time/cost limits, tool schemas + argument validation, least privilege, and human confirmation for irreversible actions.
Generate a hypothetical answer and embed that to retrieve — it is semantically closer to target documents than a short question.
When traffic is semantically repetitive (FAQs); cache by embedding similarity so paraphrases hit — needs a threshold and TTL.

column1: When an agent is the wrong choice
column2: When you need determinism, low latency, predictable cost, and debuggability — use a fixed pipeline instead.
A router sends each token to the top-k of E expert MLPs — decoupling total parameters (capacity) from per-token FLOPs.

column1: Why MoEs are harder to serve
column2: All experts must stay resident in memory, routing needs all-to-all comms, and batching is irregular.
Models use information best at the start and end of long context and worst in the middle — a U-shaped recall curve.
Position interpolation / NTK-aware / YaRN scaling to fit angles into the trained range, plus light continued pretraining.
An outcome reward model scores the final answer; a process reward model scores each reasoning step (dense feedback, catches bad process).

column1: Best-of-n accuracy with a perfect verifier
column2: 1 - (1-p)^n, where p is per-sample success probability.
Sample N chain-of-thought paths and take the majority-vote answer — no external verifier needed.
Matching image-text pairs get high cosine similarity and mismatches low — producing a shared embedding space.
Frozen vision encoder -> projector (MLP) -> LLM; projected patches become visual tokens prepended to text.
Recursively training on model-generated data loses the tails and diversity; avoid with verifiable filtering, diversity, and real-data mixing.
On a pattern [A][B]...[A] it attends to the token after the first A and predicts B — the basis of in-context learning.
Adversarial text in the input overrides the developer's instructions; the model can't separate instructions from data.
Direct: the user types it. Indirect: it is planted in third-party content (web, PDF, retrieved doc) that a normal user unknowingly triggers.

column1: Why LLMs can't separate instructions from data
column2: Everything — system prompt, user input, retrieved docs — is one uniform token stream with no privilege boundary.
Giving the model more tools, permissions, or autonomy than needed; when injected, it turns a text bug into real-world damage.
Treating model output as trusted (exec/SQL/HTML) — instead sanitize, parameterize, and sandbox it.

column1: Why indirect injection is dangerous in RAG/agents
column2: The model auto-ingests external content and can act on it via tools — enabling silent hijack and data exfiltration.
Framing/spotlighting untrusted content, input/output filters, least privilege + tool allow-lists, human confirmation, egress controls, monitoring.
Grant minimum scope: read-only where possible, narrowly-scoped credentials, rate/spend limits — to shrink the blast radius.
Deduplicate, filter/scrub PII, use DP training, add output filters, and seed canaries to measure the extraction rate.

column1: Top 3 OWASP-LLM risks for an agentic RAG app
column2: Prompt injection, excessive agency, and sensitive information disclosure.
